nameMeanings.name: Madog
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French Canadian: variant of   Madore  .

variants.name: Madore
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Eusebe, Fernand, Lucien, Normand, Adhemar, Adrien, Donat, Elzear, Francois, Gaetan, Gilles.
French Canadian: from a French shortened form of the Spanish personal name Amador, from Latin Amator (from amator ‘lover’), a Christian saint's name. Compare Mador .
In some cases possibly also Breton (Madoré): nickname meaning literally ‘good help’, from Old Breton mat ‘good’ and uuoret ‘help’.

nameMeanings.name: Goch
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Pawel, Zuzanna, Zygmunt.
North German: habitational name from any of various minor places which get their names from an ancient Germanic element goch, gog ‘marsh, bog, fen’.
Polish: from a pet form of various personal names beginning with Go-, such as Gościsław.

variants.name: Gotch
meanings: Welsh: Anglicized form of Welsh coch, goch ‘red(-haired)’; see Gooch 3.
English (Northamptonshire): variant of   Gooch   1 and 2.
Americanized form of German Götsch (see Gotsch ).

nameMeanings.name: "Kyffin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: Welsh: locative name from Gyffin (Caernarvons), recorded as Kyffyn in 1284, or perhaps Cyffin (Montgomerys). The name derives from Welsh (y) cyffin ‘border land’; see DPN Wales , p. 185.

variants.name: DPN Wales
meanings: Welsh: locative name from Gyffin (Caernarvons), recorded as Kyffyn in 1284, or perhaps Cyffin (Montgomerys). The name derives from Welsh (y) cyffin ‘border land’; see DPN Wales , p. 185.

nameMeanings.name: Madog
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Traditional name, possibly having the sense ‘fortunate’ or alternatively from Celtic aodh ‘fire’.

nameMeanings.name: Cyffin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (southwestern England, of Norman origin) and French: nickname from Middle English cofin, coffin, Old French cof(f)in (from Late Latin cophinus, Greek kophinos) ‘container, basket; coffer, chest (for keeping treasures, documents, armour, etc.)’. Early bearers of this as a hereditary surname were of knightly rank. Old French cofin was synonymous with coffer, and it may be that Cofin was used to denote a keeper of the (royal) coffer, attested in Anglo-Latin cofferarius. Compare Coffer . The modern English word coffin is a specialized development of this term, not attested until the 16th century.
History: Tristram Coffin came from Brixham, Devon, to Haverhill, MA, before 1647. An important line of his descendants is associated with Nantucket and Martha's Vineyard.

variants.name: Coffer
meanings: English: metonymic occupational name from Middle English cofre, coffre, coffer ‘box, chest’ (from Old French cof(f)rehere), used to denote a box or coffer maker (Old French coffrier), perhaps also for a treasurer.
Probably also an Americanized form of German Kaufer or Kauffer.

variants.name: Coffing
meanings: English: variant of   Coffin  .

nameMeanings.name: ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of any of the names beginning with this syllable.

variants.name: Alexander
meanings: From the Latin form of the Greek name Alexandros, from alexein ‘to defend’ + anēr ‘man, warrior’ (genitive andros). The name became extremely popular in the post-classical period, and was borne by several individuals in the New Testament and some early Christian saints. Its use as a common given name throughout Europe, however, derives largely from the fame of Alexander the Great , King of Macedon ( 356–323 bc ), around whom a large body of popular legend grew up in late antiquity, much of which came to be embodied in the medieval ‘Alexander romances’.

variants.name: Alan
meanings: Of Celtic origin and uncertain derivation (possibly a diminutive of a word meaning ‘rock’). It was introduced into England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror , most notably Alan , Earl of Brittany, who was rewarded for his services with vast estates in the newly conquered kingdom. In Britain the variants Allan and Allen are considerably less frequent, and generally represent transferred uses of surname forms, whereas in America all three forms of the name are approximately equally common. See also Alun .

variants.name: Albert
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

en.lifeSummary: When Madog Cyffin ap Madog Goch  "Kyffin was born in 1300, in Montgomery, Montgomeryshire, Wales, United Kingdom, his father, gg- son, was 31 and his mother, Mrs. gg son, was 27. He married Tangwystl verch Ieuan. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1350, in Llansilin, Denbighshire, Wales, United Kingdom, at the age of 50.
